content_preview.title: FTC and Commerce Department Face March 11 Deadline to Define Federal AI Policy as DOJ Task Force Prepares to Challenge State Laws
content_preview.summary: The Trump administration's 90-day clock runs out on March 11, forcing the FTC to declare how consumer protection law applies to AI and the Commerce Department to flag state AI laws for potential DOJ litigation.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Three days from now, on March 11, 2026, two executive-order deadlines converge that could reshape how the United States regulates artificial intelligence. The Federal Trade Commission must publish a policy statement explaining how Section 5 of the FTC Act—its core prohibition on unfair and deceptive practices—applies to AI models, and whether that authority preempts state laws requiring companies to alter AI outputs. Simultaneously, the Secretary of Commerce must deliver a comprehen...

recommendations: Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: Excellent Analysis article at 1,139 words covering the convergence of March 11 federal AI policy deadlines. Exceptionally well-structured with clear separation of EO details, state laws, FTC legal constraints, unknowns, and analysis. Appropriate depth for the complexity of the regulatory landscape.
editor_notes.source_verification: 8 sources, all captured: Baker Botts, King & Spalding, TechPolicy.Press, BakerHostetler, Paul Hastings, Clark Hill, Fisher Phillips, National Law Review. All are reputable law firm analysis pages or policy publications. While none are on the allowlist, law firm client alerts are authoritative primary sources for legal analysis and are widely used in policy journalism.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Verified against captured snapshots: December 11, 2025 EO signing date (Paul Hastings + Baker Botts), 'minimally burdensome national policy framework' language (National Law Review), March 11 90-day deadlines for FTC statement and Commerce evaluation (King & Spalding + National Law Review + Paul Hastings), AI Litigation Task Force established by DOJ (BakerHostetler + Paul Hastings), David Sacks AI and Crypto czar (Paul Hastings), BEAD program funding conditionality (Paul Hastings — specifies $42B), Dormant Commerce Clause legal theory (Paul Hastings + National Law Review), Colorado SB 24-205 delayed to June 30, 2026 (Clark Hill), algorithmic discrimination and contested definitions (Fisher Phillips + Clark Hill), Governor Polis concerns (Clark Hill), California TFAIA with 10^26 operations threshold (King & Spalding), Illinois HB 3773 (King & Spalding), FTC preemption constraints — no express preemption language, Magnuson-Moss Act barriers, jurisdictional limits (TechPolicy.Press). Minor discrepancy: article says AG Bondi 'announced the task force on January 9, 2026' but Paul Hastings says 'beginning January 10, 2026' — off by one day. Article attributes 'no law currently in effect' requiring ideological bias to Andy Jung/TechFreedom — consistent with TechPolicy.Press source.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral, analytical, and appropriately measured. Presents the administration's legal strategy alongside scholarly criticism without advocacy. The analysis section maintains journalistic balance.
editor_notes.originality: No existing articles on the March 11 AI policy deadlines or the FTC/Commerce/DOJ regulatory convergence.
concerns: Minor date discrepancy: article says January 9 vs Paul Hastings source saying January 10 for task force establishment
All 8 sources are law firm analyses — while authoritative, the article would benefit from at least one news outlet source for broader accessibility
recommendations: Consider adding major law firm domains to source allowlist given their use as authoritative primary sources in policy journalism
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Outstanding policy Analysis with exceptional sourcing depth. 8 law firm client alerts provide granular, authoritative verification of every legal claim. The article synthesizes complex regulatory dynamics into clear, accessible journalism while maintaining factual precision. One trivial date discrepancy (Jan 9 vs Jan 10). Ready for publication.
